audit_trail.notes: All three reviewers converged on a routine verdict across both cycles. The paper is self-described as pedagogical and contains no new results. The primary unresolved dispute is codex's major objection about A_r normalization in the radiation-era affine integral; claude and gemini both assessed this as a unit-convention misreading (A_r=1 implicit), but codex maintained the objection with high confidence (0.97) in cycle 2 after claiming to inspect the PDF. This disagreement is flagged as a required human spotcheck and prevents a clean technical-soundness assessment. The moderate objection about 1+1D Einstein triviality and the moderate overgenerality claim for Eq.(43) both survived cross-review and are included. Minor objections about the p=0 de Sitter edge case and the unmotivated negative-η branch also survived. The curvature formula concern was partially resolved by codex's cycle-2 PDF inspection.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper is a pedagogical walkthrough of conformal time in expanding universes, deriving how freely moving particles travel and how spacetime curves in three standard cosmological eras (radiation, matter, and vacuum domination). The review board found the paper technically competent and correct in its core results, but unanimously noted it contains no original research—all results appear in standard graduate textbooks. The most important caveat for a student reader is that the simplified 1+1-dimensional model used throughout cannot be derived from Einstein's equations in two dimensions, a fact the paper does not clearly state at the point of use.
human_spotchecks: Inspect the typeset PDF for Eqs. (16)–(20) and the repeated discussion around Eq. (48): does the paper explicitly carry A_r as a free constant throughout the radiation-era affine-parameter derivation, or does it normalize A_r=1 before integrating? This determines whether codex's major objection (wrong A_r powers) is a genuine dimensional error or a unit-convention misreading.

Verify the curvature formulas in Eqs. (30)–(33) and Appendix A Eqs. (56)–(57) against an independent 1+1D GR calculation using the paper's stated Riemann sign convention. Codex reports these are correct in the PDF; the HTML rendering is incomplete. Confirm signs and numerical coefficients.

Determine whether the matter-era affine integral ∫η⁴/√(k²+η⁴)dη admits a closed form in terms of elliptic integrals or hypergeometric functions. If a closed form exists, the paper's claim that 'exact antiderivatives are no longer elementary' is potentially misleading and Fig. 4 could display an exact result rather than a numerical one.

Check whether references [4] (Boyle, Finn, Turok 2022) and [15] (Robles-Perez 2019) are correctly cited for the stated context about time-reversal-symmetric and CPT-symmetric cosmologies in the Introduction, or whether the citations are decorative.
public_summary: "A Survey through Conformal Time" presents a step-by-step pedagogical derivation of conformal-time relations, geodesic equations, affine-parameter asymptotics, and curvature for radiation-dominated, matter-dominated, and exact de Sitter FRW cosmologies, primarily in a 1+1-dimensional setting before briefly extending to 3+1 dimensions. The paper's aim is clarity of exposition rather than new results, and it acknowledges this explicitly. The review board unanimously found the paper technically competent in its main claims: the conformal-time scalings, null geodesic straightness, affine-parameter crossover behavior, and hyperbolic de Sitter trajectories all pass independent verification. All three reviewers issued a routine verdict, noting that no new theorem, method, or physical insight is introduced and that all content appears in the cited standard textbooks. A moderate pedagogical gap—failing to warn readers that 1+1-dimensional gravity has identically vanishing Einstein tensor, so the Friedmann scale factors are kinematic inputs borrowed from 3+1D rather than solutions to any 1+1D field equation—was raised by all three reviewers and survived cross-review. A technical dispute about normalization factors in the radiation-era affine integral was not fully resolved and requires inspection of the typeset PDF.
reviewer_disagreements: The most significant disagreement concerns the A_r normalization in the radiation-era affine integral (Eqs. 17–20). Codex (cycle 2, confidence 0.97) maintains that the printed exact expression and both asymptotic coefficients are missing powers of A_r, constituting a major error in the central worked example. Claude and gemini both argue the paper implicitly normalizes A_r=1, which is standard in pedagogical treatments, and that under this convention the formulas are correct. This dispute was not resolved in two review cycles and is flagged as a required human spotcheck.

Technical soundness scores diverged substantially: gemini scored 8–9/10 across both cycles, claude scored 7–7.5/10, and codex scored 5.2–5.4/10. The gap reflects the unresolved A_r dispute: if codex is correct, the paper's central example contains a dimensional error; if claude and gemini are correct, the paper is technically sound at a routine pedagogical level.

Regarding the curvature formulas (Eqs. 30–33 and Appendix A Eqs. 56–57): claude flagged these as unverifiable from the HTML rendering and left them as a human spotcheck. Codex (cycle 2) reported inspecting the PDF directly and finding the formulas correct under the stated sign convention, effectively resolving this particular concern. Gemini did not flag the curvature formulas as problematic.

On the overgenerality of Eq.(43): codex and claude both found the 'genuinely general' claim for any spatially flat conformal metric to be overstated given Appendix A. Gemini (cycle 2) disagreed, arguing that Section VI explicitly restricts the domain to metrics of the form (1), which are spatially homogeneous, making the appendix a separate extension rather than a counterexample.

strongest_objections.objection: In 1+1-dimensional Lorentzian gravity the Einstein tensor G_{μν} vanishes identically, so the three cosmological backgrounds (radiation a∝t^{1/2}, matter a∝t^{2/3}, de Sitter a=e^{Ht}) have no dynamical justification within the 1+1D model and must be treated as kinematic inputs imported from 3+1D Friedmann dynamics. The paper does not state this at the point of use; a student could reasonably conclude the 1+1D analysis is self-consistent as a gravitational theory.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section II and throughout; briefly acknowledged only in the Conclusion

strongest_objections.objection: The claim that Eq.(43) is 'genuinely general for any spatially flat conformal metric' is overstated. The derivation relies on x being a cyclic coordinate, which requires a spatially homogeneous conformal factor a(η). Appendix A introduces a spatially dependent factor f(η,x), for which the x-geodesic equation contains ∂f/∂x terms and the conserved momentum argument fails entirely.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract; Section VI, Eqs. (42)–(43); Appendix A, Eqs. (52)–(55)

strongest_objections.objection: The radiation-era affine-parameter integral and its asymptotic coefficients may carry incorrect powers of the proportionality constant A_r. Direct integration of Eq.(16) yields A_r-dependent coefficients in both the exact expression and the small-η and large-η limits that differ from those printed in Eqs.(17)–(20). If the paper does not explicitly normalize A_r=1, the central worked example is dimensionally wrong.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section III.2, Eqs. (16)–(20); Section VI around Eq. (48)

strongest_objections.objection: The de Sitter hyperbola derivation in Eqs.(27)–(28) divides by the conserved comoving momentum p, implicitly excluding the p=0 comoving geodesic (x=constant), which is a physically distinct and important class of timelike trajectories not represented by a finite hyperbola.

The integration constant for de Sitter conformal time is chosen so that η→0⁻ as t→∞, placing the entire physical expanding patch on the negative-η branch, but the paper offers no motivation for this choice and does not discuss the nature of the conformal boundary at η=0 or the contracting (η>0) branch, which may leave a pedagogical reader confused.

strongest_supported_claims: The conformal-time scalings a∝η (radiation domination), a∝η² (matter domination), and a∝1/|η| (de Sitter) follow by elementary integration of dη=dt/a(t) for the respective power-law and exponential scale factors (Section II, Eqs. 6–8).
Null geodesics are straight lines (x=±η+const) in conformal coordinates because the scale factor cancels from the null condition, making causal structure locally Minkowskian (Section III.1, Eq. 15).

The radiation-era timelike affine parameter has an exact closed form with cubic small-η and quadratic large-η asymptotics, describing a clean crossover from momentum-dominated to scale-factor-dominated evolution (Section III.2, Eqs. 17–20).

The matter-era timelike affine parameter exhibits quintic small-η and cubic large-η asymptotics, reflecting the same momentum-to-expansion crossover with stronger late-time stretching due to the quadratic conformal growth of a(η) (Section VI, Eqs. 49–50).
Timelike geodesics in the 1+1-dimensional exact de Sitter flat patch form hyperbolae in the (η, x) conformal-coordinate plane for non-zero conserved comoving momentum (Section III.4, Eq. 28).

The de Sitter scalar curvature is constant and the radiation- and matter-dominated geometries have non-constant curvature, consistent with the maximal symmetry of exact de Sitter spacetime (Section IV, Eqs. 30–33).

The conformal factor of the de Sitter flat patch formally matches the Poincaré half-plane metric but the two are not isometric due to Lorentzian versus Euclidean signature, and geodesic families should not be identified (Section IV, Eq. 34 and surrounding discussion).
technical_takeaway: The paper correctly derives conformal-time–scale-factor relations (a∝η for radiation, a∝η² for matter, a∝1/|η| for de Sitter), geodesic first integrals from translation invariance and affine normalization, affine-parameter asymptotics (cubic/quadratic crossover in the radiation era; quintic/cubic in the matter era), and hyperbolic de Sitter timelike trajectories in the conformal coordinate plane. An unresolved dispute between reviewers concerns whether the radiation-era affine integral in Eq.(17) carries the correct powers of the proportionality constant A_r: two reviewers read the paper as implicitly normalizing A_r=1 (standard in pedagogical treatments), while one reviewer maintains the printed formulas are dimensionally wrong even under that reading. The claim that Eq.(43) is "genuinely general for any spatially flat conformal metric" is overstated: the derivation requires a spatially homogeneous conformal factor a(η) and fails for the spatially dependent f(η,x) treated in Appendix A.
